body_txt: Little old ladies love cats. Cats like climbing trees. Cats don't really like climbing down from those trees. Now that's one serious emergency situation. This trope is often used to give an opportunity for an Anti-Hero to Pet the Dog , especially if the character is a Kindhearted Cat Lover , but it can also be a sign of Chronic Hero Syndrome . Other times it is used to illustrate that the story takes place in a peaceful lovely town where this kind of thing is the biggest emergency anyone can think of. In this latter case usually firemen come to help the old lady. A common variation of this trope is that the cat really dislikes whoever's coming to rescue it and goes all Tasmanian Devil on the rescuer in a whirl of claws and enraged meow-shrieking. This is Truth in Television , if not as common as media would have you think. While cats are typically ground-dwellers, they have some rather impressive arboreal tendencies, and will regularly climb up tall objects to get a better vantage point — indoors or outdoors — and trees are no exception to the things they'll climb. The main issue cats face getting down from trees are that they can't climb down headfirst (in contrast to other arboreal creatures like squirrels or monkeys), and the stress from having to climb down without being able to see where they're going (i.e. tail first) tends to make them reluctant to move. This is especially so if there are people watching them — cats take direct, sustained eye contact as a sign of aggression, so someone looking at them, regardless of whether the person watching is a familiar face, can just further unnerve them into just staying in place for the moment. By the way, if your cat does go up a tree (or on a roof), call a tree service or your local animal control department/shelter/what have you. The fire department has better things to do. Like, you know, putting out fires. If you do call these agencies, and the cat is not in any danger or a danger to others, they'll tell you to leave it — it got up there, and it will find its way down when it gets hungry. A very common question asked by responders to these situations is: "How many times have you seen a cat skeleton in a tree?" If anything, calling the fire department is a bad idea, since the noise and smell of the approaching fire engine (sirens or not), let alone the experience of a giant stranger climbing up the tree to grab them is likely to just further stress the poor critter out — not to mention it may provoke them into attacking the first set of hands that reaches for them. Of course, if the tree is on fire, that's a different story, but then you'd probably have bigger problems on your hands. Sub-Trope of Arduous Descent to Terra Firma .
